title: Module 5 — Reading Quiz
description: 10-question reading quiz covering breastfeeding, infant nutrition, childhood energy needs, and adolescent nutrient requirements.

questions.q: What is the primary carbohydrate found in breast milk?
questions.rationale: Lactose is the primary carbohydrate in breast milk, making up almost all of its carbohydrate content. Lactose is a disaccharide composed of glucose + galactose. Importantly, lactose intolerance is practically nonexistent in infants because their small intestines produce abundant lactase to break it down efficiently. This is distinct from an allergy to cow's milk protein, which is a different condition entirely.

questions.q: Children ages 4 to 8 require _______ kcal per day, depending on their activity level and gender.
questions.rationale: Children ages 4–8 require 1,200–1,800 kcal/day (girls) to 1,200–2,000 kcal/day (boys) depending on activity level. The range that covers both is 1,200–1,800 kcal. Energy needs at this stage are lower than adolescence because the rapid growth of infancy and toddler years has slowed and puberty has not yet begun. More active children need calories toward the higher end of the range.

questions.q: Which micronutrient is especially important for girls beginning menstruation during puberty?
questions.rationale: Iron is the critical micronutrient for adolescent girls once menstruation begins. The RDA jumps to 15 mg/day for girls ages 14–18 to replace iron lost through monthly blood loss. Boys the same age only need 11 mg/day. This is one of the key sex-based differences in micronutrient needs that starts in adolescence. Iron deficiency anemia during this period can cause fatigue, impaired cognition, and reduced athletic performance.

questions.q: What is a significant contributing factor to toddler obesity?
questions.rationale: Physical inactivity (low activity levels) is a significant contributing factor to toddler obesity. Other contributing factors include time-constrained parents turning to fast food, limited access to fresh produce, and lack of breastfeeding support. The reason this matters long-term: adipose cells created during childhood persist permanently — they can shrink but never disappear once formed. This is why preventing obesity in early childhood is more effective than treating it in adulthood.

questions.q: Which micronutrient is low in breast milk but essential for blood clotting?
questions.rationale: Vitamin K is low in breast milk, and newborns are also born with very limited stores of it. Since Vitamin K is required for the synthesis of clotting factors, deficiency puts newborns at serious risk for Vitamin K Deficiency Bleeding (VKDB), which can cause life-threatening internal bleeding including intracranial hemorrhage. This is why the AAP has recommended a Vitamin K injection at birth since 1961. If a parent refuses the injection, educating about the bleeding risk is a nursing priority. Note: Vitamin D is also low in breast milk but its role is bone health, not clotting.

questions.q: Breastfed babies are less likely to develop asthma, allergies, and sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).
questions.rationale: True. Breast milk contains immunoglobulins, enzymes, immune factors, and white blood cells that actively support the infant's developing immune system. Research shows breastfed babies have significantly lower rates of asthma, allergies, ear infections, respiratory illness, GI problems, and SIDS compared to formula-fed infants. These protective benefits persist even after the infant is weaned from breast milk. The AAP and WHO both strongly recommend exclusive breastfeeding for the first 6 months of life based on this evidence.

questions.q: What is a major concern for older adults regarding body composition?
opts: Loss of muscle mass and strength
questions.rationale: Sarcopenia — the age-related loss of muscle mass and strength — is a major concern for older adults. It accelerates after age 60 and leads to increased fall risk, reduced functional independence, impaired immunity, and higher mortality. The primary nutritional intervention is increasing protein intake to 1.2–2.0 g/kg/day (higher than the standard adult RDA of 0.8 g/kg) combined with resistance exercise and adequate Vitamin D. Body fat actually tends to increase and redistribute to the abdominal area with aging, while bone mass decreases — so all three of the wrong answers are the opposite of what actually happens.

questions.q: What dietary intervention is recommended for elderly adults to combat the anorexia of aging?
opts: Increase meal frequency and add high-calorie foods
questions.rationale: The anorexia of aging is a serious condition — not a normal part of aging — where chronically poor food intake leads to dangerous weight loss. The recommended interventions are: frequent small meals throughout the day, adding calorie-dense healthy foods (nuts, avocado, whole grain pasta, potatoes), and liquid nutritional supplements between meals. Patients should be weighed weekly until improvement is shown. The goal is to maximize caloric and nutrient density in whatever the patient is willing to eat — restricting calories or fat in this population is the opposite of what is needed.

questions.q: What is the general serving size guideline for toddlers?
opts: Twice the serving size of an infant
Half the serving size of an adult
Equal to the serving size of an infant
One-quarter the serving size of an adult
questions.rationale: Toddler serving sizes should be approximately one-quarter of an adult serving, or about one tablespoon per year of life. A 2-year-old gets roughly 2 tablespoons of each food; a 3-year-old gets about 3 tablespoons. Parents often over-serve toddlers and then worry when the child doesn't finish — understanding age-appropriate portions reduces unnecessary mealtime conflict. This also ties to Ellyn Satter's Division of Responsibility: the parent provides appropriate portions, and the child decides how much to eat.

questions.q: During the middle-aged years, preventive nutrition practices such as consuming antioxidants and omega-3 fatty acids can reduce disease risk.
questions.rationale: True. Preventive nutrition is defined as dietary practices directed toward reducing disease and promoting health and well-being. During the middle-aged years specifically: colorful fruits and vegetables provide antioxidants that reduce oxidative stress linked to cancer and cardiovascular disease, and omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish (salmon, mackerel, tuna) help prevent coronary artery disease by reducing triglycerides and inflammation. Adequate B6, B12, and folate are also recommended during this life stage to control homocysteine — a metabolic byproduct that damages arterial walls when elevated.
